Avoids inappropriate therapies (e.g., antibiotics without infection, chemotherapy/radiation as primary therapy for dermoid/epidermoid cysts) and does not claim medical therapy can definitively remove the cyst.

Uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions, and discusses risks/benefits and monitoring for hormone replacement (e.g., sodium monitoring with desmopressin; free T4 monitoring in central hypothyroidism).

If prescribing sex hormone therapy, assesses pregnancy intention/fertility goals and screens for major contraindications (e.g., thromboembolic disease, hormone-sensitive malignancy) before recommending.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and briefly distinguishes acute vs chronic insomnia and common contributors (stress, substances, medical/psychiatric comorbidity).
Elicits core insomnia features: difficulty initiating sleep, difficulty maintaining sleep, early morning awakening, frequency, duration, and daytime impairment/distress.

Screens for secondary causes and key differentials: depression/anxiety, bipolar symptoms, substance/caffeine/alcohol use, medication-induced insomnia (stimulants, SSRIs, steroids), circadian rhythm disorder, restless legs symptoms, and obstructive sleep apnea red flags (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness).

Assesses safety risks before any sedative-hypnotic recommendation: fall risk, older age/frailty, pregnancy status if relevant, history of substance use disorder, prior overdose, and suicidal ideation/self-harm risk.

Meets required safety checks before prescribing: explicitly checks allergies, potential drug-drug interactions (including other CNS depressants/opioids/benzodiazepines), and contraindications (e.g., severe hepatic impairment for certain agents, untreated sleep apnea/respiratory compromise, pregnancy/lactation considerations, history of complex sleep behaviors with zolpidem, myasthenia gravis).
Explains treatment and must include both "CBT-I" as first-line and "sedative-hypnotics" as time-limited adjuncts when indicated; avoids presenting medications as sole therapy.

Provides specific CBT-I elements (at least two): stimulus control, sleep restriction/time-in-bed consolidation, cognitive restructuring, relaxation training, and sleep hygiene as supportive (not standalone).

If prescribing, selects an appropriate agent and avoids high-risk choices (e.g., avoids benzodiazepines in older adults; avoids antipsychotics for primary insomnia; avoids long-term sedative-hypnotic use).

Counsels on medication adverse effects and precautions: next-day impairment, falls, parasomnias/complex sleep behaviors, avoiding alcohol/other sedatives, and not driving/operating machinery if drowsy.
Sets a clear follow-up timeframe (e.g., 2-4 weeks) with measurable goals (sleep onset latency, wake after sleep onset, total sleep time, daytime function) and a plan to taper/stop sedative-hypnotics.
Uses shared decision-making, validates patient experience, and provides return precautions (worsening mood, suicidality, severe adverse effects, confusion, falls, breathing problems at night).
